I'm sure I would have gotten the revealer, even if not the application of it, much sooner had it been better clued – "forgo" means to do without, but the connotation is of necessity, not rejection. Renouncing implies a level of intentionality. You forgo something because you can't have it all and something has to give. You renounce something because you have chosen to distance yourself from it.

I was going to complain about the "fake" singular of "Kudo".  It comes from the Greek κῦδος meaning "fame" or "glory", and it is singular, not plural.  There is no word "kudo"!

However, the fine folks at Merriam-Webster remind me that language changes over time, and that "back formation" is a common way for new words to enter the language.
So, if you use the word "pea" (which comes from the back formation of "pease" from the Latin pisum) or cherry (back formation from the French "c(h)erise"), you have no right to complain about "kudo".

BUT I WANT TO COMPLAIN ABOUT KUDO!!!

As a very experienced solver, I did not find this puzzle any harder than other Thursdays recently; in fact, I exceeded my recent Thursday average by about 16%. And a lot of the regulars here seem to be reporting something similar—a challenge, but not overly difficult. 

This seems to belie what someone hypothesized a few days ago, that the results skew toward the more experienced because they are the ones who most likely self-selected by linking to xwstats. 

In any event, I will reiterate that when the site says that the median solve time is 15:46, that’s not the median for all solvers. It is only the self-selected group who joined the site. Newcomers should look at it like par in golf. It’s the score experienced players should be able to get, not the weekend duffer. But it’s still useful to determine difficulty. And surprisingly accurate.

I may add that the more experienced I get, the harder it is to gauge difficulty level for newer solvers. That makes it awesome that the editors can do so on a regular basis with so little error.

A good theme is like a good punchline. It should give you pause, then snap into place and make you feel a bit silly for not seeing it coming.

This did neither of those for me. Moreover—just like a joke—if it takes a full paragraph to explain it, it probably wasn’t all that good.

How was the solver supposed to know which letters to remove? Nothing in the puzzle indicated you should remove the same four letters. Even after getting the solve from crosses, I kept scratching my head trying to remove the first four, last four, and fourth letters.

Conceding defeat and turning to wordplay left me with a feeling of “Oh. Huh. Sure, I guess.”
